---
title: FAQ and troubleshooting
description: Error messages an agent-consumer hits when connecting to a vault, and how to fix each one.
---

This page maps the messages anima-mcp prints back to a fix. Each entry is a
trigger (what you see) and the step that clears it. The messages are reproduced
verbatim so an agent can pattern-match on them.

If you have not paired yet, start with the [quickstart](/build/quickstart): the
Anima app generates the agent's own key, registers it on your vault, and hands
back a config snippet that fills the env values below. There is one pairing path,
the in-app wizard, so this page does not re-describe it; it just maps each
message to its fix.

## Configuration

### Missing or invalid env

**Trigger.** The process exits at startup with:

```
anima-mcp is not paired: missing or invalid env: ANIMA_AGENT_KEY (suiprivkey…), ANIMA_VAULT_ID (0x… vault object id), ANIMA_OWNER_ADDRESS (0x… wallet address).
Open the ANIMA app → Settings → Connect external agent to generate the agent key,
register it on your vault, and copy the config snippet (it fills these values).
```

Only the values that are actually missing or malformed are listed. Three are
required:

- `ANIMA_AGENT_KEY` must be present and start with `suiprivkey`. A value in
  some other format (a `0x…` address pasted by mistake, a key from another
  tool) does not start with `suiprivkey`, so it lands here, in this message,
  not in the parse error below.
- `ANIMA_VAULT_ID` must be a `0x…` hex object id.
- `ANIMA_OWNER_ADDRESS` must be a `0x…` hex wallet address.

**Fix.** Open the Anima app, go to Settings, choose Connect external agent, and
copy the config snippet. It fills all three (plus an optional agent name). Paste
the values into your agent's MCP config exactly as given.

### Agent key is not a suiprivkey

**Trigger.** The key is present but cannot be parsed:

```
ANIMA_AGENT_KEY is not a valid suiprivkey: copy it exactly from the ANIMA app pairing screen.
```

This is distinct from the missing-env message above. The value starts with
`suiprivkey`, so it passes the env check, but it does not fully decode into a
usable key. The usual cause is a partial paste: a key that is truncated,
clipped, or otherwise corrupted, so the prefix is right but the length or
checksum is wrong. A value that is not in `suiprivkey` form at all does not
reach this point; it produces the missing-env message above instead.

**Fix.** Copy `ANIMA_AGENT_KEY` again from the pairing screen, whole, with no
leading or trailing characters. The agent key is the MCP process's own keypair;
it is generated for you and lives only in your local config.

## Pairing and access

### Unpaired or revoked key

**Trigger.** The key parses but is not on the vault's allowlist:

```
Agent key not paired: 0x<agent-address> is not an allowlisted agent of vault 0x<vault-id>. Register it in the ANIMA app (Settings → Connect external agent), then retry. If it was paired before, the key may have been revoked: generate and pair a fresh one.
```

The check runs against the vault's on-chain allowlist when the process first
touches the vault, so a paired key that was later revoked produces the same
message.

**Fix.** In the Anima app, Settings, Connect external agent, register this agent
address on the vault, then restart the agent. If the key was paired before and
got revoked, it cannot be re-enabled. Generate a fresh agent key and pair that
one.

<Callout type="info">
The agent key is a full-vault read and write credential, not a per-note grant.
A paired agent can read and write every note in the vault. There is no narrower
scope; treat pairing a key like handing out a working copy of the vault. To take
that access away, revoke the key from the same place you paired it: the Anima
app, Settings, Connect external agent, where the vault's agents are listed.
Revoking removes the key from the vault's on-chain allowlist. It does not stop a
running agent on its next call; see
[Revocation](#revocation-takes-effect-on-the-next-process-start) for the timing.
</Callout>

### Vault not found on testnet

**Trigger.** The vault id does not resolve on chain:

```
Vault 0x<vault-id> not found on testnet: check ANIMA_VAULT_ID.
```

**Fix.** Confirm `ANIMA_VAULT_ID` matches the vault you mean. Re-copy it from the
pairing snippet in the app. A wrong network or a stale id from an earlier vault
both land here. The id must be the vault object id, not the owner address.

## Funding

Anima writes notes to Walrus. A write spends a little SUI for gas and a little
WAL for storage, paid by the agent's own address. Before each write the agent
runs a balance preflight; if it cannot afford the write, it stops there rather
than failing partway through.

### Unfunded agent

**Trigger.** The preflight fails before the write:

```
Agent address 0x<agent-address> cannot afford this write, SUI: <n> MIST (needs ≥ 0.1 SUI), WAL: <n> FROST (needs ≥ 0.02 WAL). Fund it with testnet SUI (faucet) and exchange some for WAL, then retry.
```

The two thresholds the preflight enforces:

- **SUI:** at least `0.1 SUI` (`100_000_000` MIST), held as a gas reserve.
- **WAL:** at least `0.02 WAL` (`20_000_000` FROST), enough for dozens of writes.

The message only flags the side you are short on, so you may see just the SUI
clause, just the WAL clause, or both.

<Callout type="warn">
The write path does not auto-swap SUI for WAL. Funding SUI alone does not unblock
the write. You have to acquire WAL yourself before retrying.
</Callout>

**Fix.** Two steps:

1. Send the agent address testnet SUI (from the faucet, or any wallet that has
   some).
2. Swap a small amount of that SUI for WAL on the official testnet exchange (the
   same swap as `walrus get-wal`), so the WAL lands on the agent address.

Then retry the write. Because the preflight reads live balances, the next
attempt clears once both thresholds are met.

### Faucet per-IP rate limit

**Trigger.** The testnet SUI faucet rejects the request because too many calls
came from the same IP in a short window. The faucet is per-IP rate-limited, so
repeated runs from one machine or one CI runner hit the cap quickly.

**Fix.** Do not loop on the faucet. Instead, reuse a balance you already have:

- Send SUI to the agent from another wallet that is already funded, rather than
  asking the faucet again.
- For demos and CI, use a pre-funded wallet and top the agent up from it. A
  funded agent address holds its balance across runs, so one funding pass is
  usually enough for a long session.

## Staleness

The agent keeps a local index of the vault and refreshes it on a timer rather
than on every call. That keeps reads fast, and it means there are a few moments
where what the agent sees and what is on chain differ. Three of them are worth
knowing as separate cases.

### Index freshness window

The index has a 60-second freshness window. A read (search, list, get) is served
from the cached index for up to 60 seconds before the agent rebuilds it from
chain. So a note written by a different writer can take up to a minute to appear
in this agent's reads, even though it is already on chain.

If you need the current state right now, the simplest lever is time: wait out the
window and the next read rebuilds.

### Write-through updates only the writer's own cache

When this agent writes a note, it patches the new note straight into its own
in-memory and on-disk index (a write-through), so the writer sees its own note
immediately. That update goes to the writer's own index. A
second agent connected to the same vault will not see the new note until that
agent's own freshness window lapses (within 60 seconds) and its index refreshes.

This matters when two agents collaborate on one vault: the writer is consistent
with itself instantly, but the reader is consistent only after its own refresh.

### A deleted note can briefly survive in a stale index

Deletes are wallet-signed and run on chain (survivors are rewritten, then the old
blob is removed). An agent whose cached index predates that delete can still
return the note from search, list, or get until its freshness window lapses and
it rebuilds. So for up to the same 60-second window, a note that is gone on chain
may still be served from a stale index.

If a read returns a note you know was deleted, it is this case. Let the index
refresh, then read again.

<Callout type="info">
None of these is a write-loss. The chain is the source of truth in every case;
the index is a disposable cache that converges on it within the freshness window.
There is no realtime-conflict guarantee here, only an upper bound on how stale a
read can be.
</Callout>

## Revocation takes effect on the next process start

Pairing is checked when the agent process first touches the vault, and the result
is held for the lifetime of that process. So when you revoke an agent's key, a
process that is already running does not lose access on its next call. It keeps
working with the access it checked at startup until it restarts, and then the
pairing check fails (see [Unpaired or revoked key](#unpaired-or-revoked-key)).

**Implication.** Revoking is necessary but, for an already-running agent, not
instantaneous. To cut off a running agent immediately, revoke the key and stop
the agent process. On its next start it cannot re-pair.

## Pairing funds the agent in two steps

A freshly paired agent is not yet able to write, even though it is allowlisted.
Funding happens in two parts, and they are not the same currency:

1. The owner funds the agent with SUI (gas).
2. The agent acquires WAL by swapping some of that SUI (storage).

Because the write path does not auto-swap, an agent that has SUI but no WAL will
pass pairing and then fail its very first write on the WAL threshold (see
[Unfunded agent](#unfunded-agent)). This is expected on a brand-new agent: fund
SUI, swap a little for WAL, and the first write goes through.
